comments:
Summary
This paper proposes an unsupervised approach to learning interest point detectors by training a neural network to produce a repeatable ranking of image patches. Top/bottom quantiles of this ranking (optionally combined with non-maximum suppression in scale-space) are used as interest points. The objective enforces that pairwise orderings of corresponding patch pairs are preserved under transformations, implemented via a hinge loss over quadruples. The method is evaluated in three settings: (i) learning from ground-truth 3D-projected correspondences (DTU→Oxford VGG), (ii) a fully-unsupervised setting using random warps/illumination, and (iii) a cross-modal RGB/depth detector trained on NYUv2. Results show improved or comparable repeatability to DoG and some improvements when combined with SIFT descriptors.

Strengths
- Clear and principled formulation: framing detection as learning to rank with repeatability constraints is elegant and mathematically well motivated (Observations 1 and 2 support the choice of top/bottom quantiles and NMS).
- Unsupervised angle is compelling: avoiding human labeling and, in the cross-modal case, learning detectors where heuristic hand-designed filters fail is an interesting contribution.
- Diverse experiments: the paper evaluates on standard benchmarks and a challenging cross-modal setup, demonstrating practical value.
- Implementation details (network architectures, optimization, augmentation strategies) are reasonably well described, enabling reproducibility (though code availability is not stated).

Weaknesses and suggestions
- Limited baselines: experiments compare primarily with DoG. While DoG is a sensible and standard baseline, the paper should include comparisons to other learned detectors or supervised methods (e.g., TILDE, LIFT, or other modern detectors), or at least discuss them quantitatively where possible. This would better position the contribution relative to prior learning-based work.
- Ablation and sensitivity analysis are limited: the effect of design choices (hinge margin, patch size, number of quadruples, different network capacities, types/amounts of augmentation, NMS neighborhood size) is not explored in depth. Such ablations would strengthen the empirical claims and guide future use.
- Runtime and resource requirements are not reported: training time, inference speed, and memory footprint are important for practical deployment of detectors in vision pipelines.
- Evaluation mostly focuses on repeatability and a basic matching score with SIFT. It would be helpful to evaluate downstream performance in complete tasks (e.g., pose estimation, SfM reconstruction quality) to demonstrate practical benefits beyond repeatability.
- The theoretical assumption of one-to-one correspondences and equal indexing in transformed objects is a simplification; discussion of how the method copes with occlusions, missing correspondences, or non-unique matches would be useful.
- Cross-modal experiment is promising but small-scale (40 frames). Larger-scale evaluation or demonstration of real application benefits (e.g., colorizing point clouds) would increase impact.

Presentation and clarity
Overall the paper is well organized and explanations are clear. Mathematical notation is concise and the optimization objective is easy to follow. A few more qualitative visualizations (e.g., distribution of responses, failure cases) and clearer statements about code/data release would improve usability.

Recommendation for authors
- Add comparisons to other learned detectors or supervised alternatives and discuss limitations when direct comparisons are infeasible.
- Provide ablation studies for key hyperparameters and architecture choices.
- Report computational costs (training and inference) and, if possible, release code/models.
- Evaluate or at least discuss the method’s behavior under occlusions and partial correspondences.
- Consider joint learning of descriptor and detector as mentioned, or provide preliminary results showing compatibility with modern learned descriptors.

Overall assessment
This paper presents a novel and well-motivated unsupervised approach to interest point detection with convincing initial experiments, especially for the challenging cross-modal case. The main limitations are in the breadth of empirical comparisons and ablation analyses. With those additions the work would be of strong interest to the computer vision community.
